ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chicken 65. FOODON_03302051 (fried chicken food product) is the closest parent category. A specific term for Indian spiced deep-fried chicken has not been minted.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific FoodEx2 term exists for Chicken 65 as a marinated deep-fried preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Chicken 65 or equivalent Indian fried chicken preparation.

allergens: gluten (cornflour/rice flour coating may contain traces; some recipes use wheat flour)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Chicken 65 is approximately 200 g, comprising 4–6 pieces of deep-fried marinated chicken, often garnished with fried curry leaves and sliced onions.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken 65 (deep-fried spiced chicken) — derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods: Chicken (cooked, fried) with adjustment for marinade and frying oil absorption
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for cooked fried chicken. Chicken 65 involves deep-frying marinated chicken with yogurt, spices, and a flour coating. Values for energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate are adapted from ICMR-NIN fried chicken data (approximately 215 kcal/100g, protein 18g, fat 14g, carbohydrate 4g per 100g) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, fried' (NDB 05064) which reports 246 kcal, 29.8g protein, 13.2g fat, 1.6g carbohydrate per 100g. A blended estimate is used reflecting the marinade and coating contribution to carbohydrate and the frying oil absorption. Final adopted values: energy 218 kcal, protein 20.5g, fat 13.5g, carbohydrate 5.2g per 100g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed fried chicken preparations — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried chicken and spiced meat preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken 65 in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. Chicken 65 is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate deep-fried preparation. The GI is estimated at 45 (low category) based on analogy with similar fried spiced chicken preparations. The low carbohydrate content (5.2g/100g, primarily from flour coating and yogurt marinade) and high protein content suppress glycemic response. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (45 × 10.4g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.7, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg/100g; ~840 mg per 200g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A 200g restaurant serving of Chicken 65 contributes approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 37% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional salt or high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index (~45)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken 65 has a low glycemic index (estimated 45) and low glycemic load per serving (~4.7), making it a relatively suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as rice or naan, and when portion size is controlled to manage total fat and calorie intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from deep-frying and total fat content (~13.5g fat, ~3.2g saturated fat per 100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-frying process in Chicken 65 contributes to elevated total fat and saturated fat content; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency, prefer air-fried or baked variants where available, and be mindful of the cumulative saturated fat contribution (~6.4g per 200g serving) relative to the recommended daily limit of 20g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken 65 is a popular South Indian deep-fried chicken dish originating from Hyderabad (Telangana/Andhra Pradesh), India, widely believed to have been created at the Buhari Hotel in Chennai in 1965, though the exact origin of the name remains debated. The dish consists of bone-in or boneless chicken pieces marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture containing red chilli powder, ginger-garlic paste, turmeric, cumin, coriander, garam masala, lemon juice, and sometimes cornflour or rice flour as a coating agent, then deep-fried in hot oil until crispy and golden-red. The result is intensely spiced, crispy on the outside, and juicy on the inside, with a characteristic deep-red colour from chilli and food colouring in some commercial preparations. Chicken 65 is served as a starter or snack, often garnished with fried curry leaves, green chillies, and sliced onions. It is a staple at Indian restaurants across Singapore, Malaysia, and the global South Asian diaspora. Nutritionally, Chicken 65 is a high-protein (approximately 20.5g/100g), moderate-fat (13.5g/100g) dish with low carbohydrate content (5.2g/100g), making it relatively suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The sodium content is notable (~420mg/100g) due to the spice marinade and seasoning. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), and the glycemic load per standard 200g serving is approximately 4.7. Clinically, it is a moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to deep-frying fat content, but low concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and high protein content.
common_user_queries: What is the difference between Chicken 65 and fried chicken?
Can I eat Chicken 65 on a low-carb diet?

confidence_reason: No exact Chicken 65 entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN fried chicken data and USDA SR Legacy fried chicken (NDB 05064), adjusted for the marinade and coating contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework for similar fried spiced chicken preparations. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides a direct Chicken 65 nutrient profile.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on ICMR-NIN (2017) fried chicken data and USDA SR Legacy NDB 05064, adjusted for yogurt marinade and flour coating carbohydrate contribution and deep-frying oil absorption. A direct Chicken 65 entry is not available in any approved primary source. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy source data for this preparation. 3. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken data (NDB 05064) and are marked as adapted. 4. The cuisine field has been corrected from 'Mexican' (input error) to 'Indian', as Chicken 65 is an Indian dish. The field survey listing under a Mexican-Indian fusion restaurant does not change the dish's origin. 5. GI is estimated at 45 (low) based on analogy with similar fried spiced chicken preparations from the University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct GI measurement for Chicken 65 was found in peer-reviewed literature. 6. Halal status is marked true as the dish is traditionally prepared with halal chicken in Indian Muslim and South Asian restaurant contexts; however, individual restaurant compliance should be verified. 7. The input cuisine tags (Mexican, Taco, Burrito, Naan) reflect the restaurant's mixed menu and are not representative of Chicken 65 itself; they have been discarded for this record.
